ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'warm water' entry; closest parent is water (food) FOODON_03301737.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00FL covers water generically; no specific warm-water sub-code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 11713004 is the substance concept for water; no temperature-specific food concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of warm water, approximately 240 ml, served at a temperature between 40–60 °C.

nutrient_source.title: Water, tap, drinking — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain drinking water nutrient profile used. Warming does not alter the nutrient composition of water; all macronutrients remain zero. Values confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for water.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Water — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water contains no digestible carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic response. By established nutritional convention, water is assigned a GI of effectively zero. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Warm water contains approximately 4 mg sodium per 100 g, making it entirely safe and beneficial for individuals managing hypertension; adequate daily water intake supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Warm water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic load of zero, making it the ideal beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes to maintain hydration without affecting blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Warm water is free of saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol, and contains only trace sodium, making it a cardiovascular-neutral beverage that supports healthy circulation and hydration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warm water is plain drinking water that has been heated to a temperature typically between 40 °C and 60 °C, below boiling point. It contains no macronutrients — no calories, protein, fat, or carbohydrates — and is nutritionally identical to room-temperature water except for its thermal state. Warm water is consumed across many cultures for comfort, digestion support, and hydration. In Filipino café settings such as Lucky Cafe in Singapore, it is offered as a simple, affordable beverage option. Clinically, warm water is safe for all dietary conditions including hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, as it contributes no glycemic load, no sodium burden, and no lipid content. It is universally halal, kosher, vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free. Warming does not alter the mineral composition of water; trace amounts of sodium, potassium, calcium, and magnesium may be present depending on the water source.
common_user_queries: Is warm water good for diabetics?
Is warm water safe for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of water?
Can I drink warm water on a diet?

confidence_reason: Warm water is plain water with a well-established and universally agreed nutrient profile of zero macronutrients. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy provides an exact match for drinking water. No estimation or analogy is required for nutrient values. GI convention for water is universally accepted.
notes: All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are naturally zero — warm water is pure water containing no energy-yielding nutrients by nature; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831). saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamins are naturally zero as water contains no lipids, carbohydrates, or organic micronutrients. sodium_mg (4 mg/100 g), potassium_mg (1 mg/100 g), calcium_mg (5 mg/100 g), and magnesium_mg (1 mg/100 g) reflect typical trace mineral content of tap drinking water per USDA SR Legacy. water_g is 100.0 as this food is entirely water. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. The food is listed under Filipino cuisine in the field survey source because it is served at a Filipino café; however, warm water is a universal beverage with no specific regional origin. Multilingual names provided per Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrients are confirmed zero or trace per USDA source.
